title: Today's AI Brief: How Endava builds an agentic organization with Codex
summary: Endava uses Codex to build an agentic organization, and the future of IVF tech.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you, both from the world of artificial intelligence. First up, we're looking at how Endava is using Codex to accelerate software delivery. My read is that this is a big deal for companies looking to streamline their development processes.

Endava, a software development company, has started using Codex to build what's called an agentic organization. What does that mean? Essentially, they're automating parts of their software development process, which means they can deliver software faster and with less manual labor. According to OpenAI, Endava is using Codex to reduce requirements analysis from weeks to just hours. That's a significant speedup. I mean, who doesn't love getting things done faster?

Also making headlines is The Download, a daily newsletter from MIT Technology Review. Their latest issue covers the intersection of AI and IVF technology. It's fascinating to see how AI is being applied to improve fertility treatments. For instance, AI can help analyze large amounts of data to optimize treatment plans. My take is that this is just one example of how AI is expanding into new areas of our lives, making a real impact on people's lives.

How Endava builds an agentic organization with Codex is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Learn how Endava uses Codex to build an agentic organization, accelerating software delivery and reducing requirements analysis from weeks to hours. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

The Download: keeping up with AI, and the future of IVF is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: This is today's edition of The Download , our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what's going on in the world of technology. Stay on top of what’s going on in AI this summer Here at MIT Technology Review, we understand exactly how relentless the pace of news from the world of artificial intelligence feels . New models and capabilities crop up ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
